content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 2, 1990


Rodney Mims
Albrecht, Mims & Battle
1440 Lincoln Street
Oroville, CA 95965-4857

Re: Your Request for
Confirmation of
Telephone Advice
Our File No. I 90-615

Dear Mr. Mims:

This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice I provided you on September 19, 1990, regarding
the contribution limits and reporting requirements of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 You represent Oroville mayoral
candidate Buss Roberts. With the exceptions noted below, your
letter accurately reflects my advice.

The Act requires that campaign statements contain the name,
address, occupation, and employer of each contributor who
contributes over $100 to the campaign. (Section 84211.) Although
the campaign statement must contain the total amount of
contributions received that were less than $100 (Section
84420(d)), there is no requirement that the statement identify
these small contributors by name. Candidates must, however,
maintain records of all contributions over $25. (Regulation
18401, copy enclosed.)

Since I spoke with you, the contribution limits in the
Political Reform Act have been declared unconstitutional. Service
Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, CLC, et al. v. FPPC, (E.D.

_________________________
1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Calif. 1990) _____ F.Supp._____. Accordingly, state law
currently provides no limits for local candidates.2

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit a
public official from participating in a governmental decision in
which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.
(Section 87100.) An official has a financial interest in a
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on the official, a member of his
or her immediate family, any business entity or real property in
which the official has an investment of $1,000 or more, or any
source of income or gifts totaling $250 or more. Income and gifts
do not include contributions. (Section 82030 and 82028.)
Therefore, the mere fact that a person has given a campaign
contribution to the mayor will not require the mayor to disqualify
himself from a governmental decision affecting that person.3

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Peggy Bernardy
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:PB:dg

2     Local ordinances establishing limits were not challenged in
the federal lawsuit, however, and you should consult with the city
attorney concerning the current viability of any local ordinances.

3     Please be aware, however, that if the mayor is serving as an
appointed member of a governmental agency, a contribution of $250
could bar him from participating in certain governmental
decisions. (Section 84308.)
